title: Results
text:
The model has been trained for around 5 hours on 2 NVIDIA L40S GPUs.
The final validation losses of the model are as follows:



Modality
Validation CE
Comment




loss
3.26



scene_desc
0.29
Low-entropy text target


tok_sam@256
1.63
Competitive among visual modalities


tok_normal@256
4.13
Harder structured visual prediction


tok_depth@256
4.27
Harder structured visual prediction


tok_rgb@256
5.98
Highest complexity among targets



We observe a relatively low loss for the SAM modality. This is partly explained by the simplicity of segmentation masks in synthetic CLEVR scenes and by the high frequency of highly predictable padding tokens required to reach the fixed 256-token length, which lowers the entropy of the prediction task compared with dense visual modalities such as RGB.



Eval loss





Training loss

title: Results
text:
Quantitative summary
The final project evaluation includes both segmentation overlap metrics and image-distribution metrics.

Segmentation quality
We scored RGB -> SAM generations using mean Intersection over Union.








Metric
Value
Notes




Mean IoU (all masks)
0.845 ± 0.113
Across 26,494 evaluated masks


Mean IoU (per-mask run)
0.844 ± 0.113
Across 26,534 evaluated masks


mIoU (per-image mean IoU)
0.855 ± 0.058
Across 5,000 images


Min IoU
0.130–0.131
Worst-case masks remain poor


Max IoU
1.000
Best cases are perfect



These IoU numbers show that the model often captures the coarse object layout reasonably well on synthetic CLEVR scenes. At the same time, the relatively large standard deviation and low minimum IoU indicate that performance is uneven across examples, with some masks reconstructed much worse than others.
A degradation in mask generation quality is observable for objects in close proximity or within images containing a large number of them. While better results are visible for low-density scenes with fewer, well-separated instances, the model clearly struggles with high occlusion and complex spatial overlaps.



RGB to SAM generations (RGB from the original PNGs, not the RGB tokens)




FID








FID comparison
Value
Interpretation




GT vs decoded GT
10.220
Distortion introduced by tokenizer/decoder alone


Decoded GT tokens vs decoded predictions
51.241
Main reported evaluation FID over 4,000 samples


Decoded GT tokens vs decoded predictions
47.888
Same evaluation over 5,000 samples


Raw GT vs predictions (in-memory Inception)
79.017
Strong overall gap in image distribution



The most meaningful FID for the model itself is the comparison between decoded ground-truth tokens and decoded predictions, because it partially factors out reconstruction loss caused by the tokenizer. Even under that fairer comparison, the FID remains high, so the generated outputs are still noticeably worse than the target distribution.
SAM tokens obtain a higher FID direct chaining (modality -> RGB) result compared to geometric modalities.




FID (Image Gen)




Normal -> RGB
38.279


Depth -> RGB
42.073


SAM -> RGB
53.863


Scene description -> RGB
61.638



Cross-modal chained generations involving SAM perform comparably to chains using geometric modalities. In particular, Normal → SAM → RGB achieves the best FID among the tested chains, suggesting that segmentation can contribute useful structural cues for image synthesis.



Generation Chain
FID

title: Results
text:
Visual results
Here are qualitative examples of chain reconstruction and generation produced by our model.

title: Results
text:
Interpretation



Examples of generated masks. First row: original cropped RGB image. Second row: SAMv2.1 Small AMG masked image. Third row, model-generated SAM masks


The quantitative picture is mixed. On one hand, IoU and mIoU suggest that the model often recovers useful segmentation structure; on the other hand, FID and the visual-token losses show that the generated outputs are still far from high-quality image synthesis.
This gap is not surprising for several reasons:

The model is small relative to modern multimodal foundation models.
Training lasts only 38’147 steps, which is a short run for a five-modality setup.
CLEVR is synthetic and structurally simple, so IoU can look decent even when boundaries and fine appearance details are imperfect.
Part of the image quality loss comes from the tokenizer-decoder pipeline itself, as shown by the non-zero FID of 10.220 between raw ground truth and decoded ground truth.
SAM sequences are padded and truncated to fit a fixed representation, which can hurt fine mask fidelity.


The fairest conclusion is that the extension is functionally successful but not yet strong in absolute quality. The model clearly learns to integrate SAM as a fifth modality and produces meaningful segmentation outputs, but the final generations remain limited by model scale, training budget, and tokenizer bottlenecks.

title: Method
text:
Extending the model with segmentation instances requires an accurately segmented and tokenized dataset aligned with the already present modalities.
For this purpose, we extend the multiCLEVR dataset from EPFL’s COM-304 course, which is composed of 50’000 training and 5’000 validation scenes, each rendered with 10 augmentations aligned across modalities.
The dataset contains geometrically simple synthetic scenes, which makes it a convenient setting for studying segmentation token generation in a controlled multimodal setup.






NoteDataset Access



The full dataset used in this project is available on Google Drive.

title: Method
text:
Extending the model with segmentation instances requires an accurately segmented and tokenized dataset aligned with the already present modalities.
For this purpose, we extend the multiCLEVR dataset from EPFL’s COM-304 course, which is composed of 50’000 training and 5’000 validation scenes, each rendered with 10 augmentations aligned across modalities.
The dataset contains geometrically simple synthetic scenes, which makes it a convenient setting for studying segmentation token generation in a controlled multimodal setup.






NoteDataset Access



The full dataset used in this project is available on Google Drive.

title: Method
text:
Labeling & Tokenization
The RGB dataset is pseudo-labeled with SAM v2.1 Small. Each augmented RGB image is sampled on a fixed 16×16 grid to extract instance masks, bounding boxes, and coordinates, then filtered with an IoU threshold of 0.94, a stability threshold of 0.96, and an NMS threshold of 0.5.
The filtered masks are processed with RoI Align to obtain 64×64 square binary masks, then resized to 256×256 to align with the other modalities. Each mask is passed through the pre-trained 4M SAM instance tokenizer (1k - 64), producing 16 flattened mask tokens.
The resulting representation for each segmentation instance contains 22 tokens: 2 coordinate tokens, 4 bounding-box tokens in XYXY format, and 16 mask tokens.
To avoid index collisions, the coordinate and bounding-box tokens are shifted by offsets on top of the 1024-sized segmentation codebook. This is a bookkeeping convention rather than an architectural change, but it helps the model distinguish the different token types more clearly.
The flattened instance sequence is then padded or sliced to a fixed length of 256 tokens using padding token 2561, resulting in a final overall codebook size of 2562.
The token layout for each instance is:

Tokenization pipeline for train sample 20


Labeling and Tokenization took around 25 hours on 2 NVIDIA L40s GPUs.

Mask quality
We observe that reconstructed masks tend to have imprecise edges and larger uncovered areas because information is lost during resizing and tokenization into a lower-dimensional latent space. This effect is more pronounced for larger masks.
We also observe occasional artifacts on object faces and shadows, which introduce noise into the tokenized segmentation data.



Examples of tokenized masks. First row: original cropped RGB image. Second row: SAMv2.1 Small AMG masked image. Third row: Our tokenized & detokenized mask


Observing the coverage ratio (\(1.0\) ideal, low is worse) and the redundancy index (\(1.0\) ideal, high is worse), we obtain decent results, showing that the instance masks generally cover most of the image and there is relatively little overlap between them.
\[
\text{Coverage Ratio} = \frac{\text{Area of Union of All Masks}}{\text{Total Image Area}}
\]
\[
\text{Redundancy index} = \frac{\sum{\text{Individual Mask Areas}}}{\text{Area of Union of All Masks}}
\]

Because the representation is capped at 256 tokens, scenes with too many instances may lose some objects after flattening. In practice, the vast majority of images contain between 1 and 11 masks, while only about 1–2% of images exceed 12 masks and are therefore affected by truncation.



Number of instance masks per image

title: Method
text:
Training setup
The reported 5-modality model is trained jointly from scratch, using a lazy aligned dataloader with SAM treated as an additional masked prediction target within the same multimodal framework. Nano4M uses a shared ViT-style encoder and per-modality decoder heads. The SAM decoder follows the same structure as the existing heads: a small transformer with cross-attention to the encoder, followed by a linear projection over the SAM vocabulary.



Config
Value




Total Tokens
5B


Warmup Tokens
500M


Optimizer
AdamW

[0.9, 0.95]

0.0006 - 0.000001

1.0

PyTorch DDP

256 per GPU

38’146

title: Overview
text: A COM-304 project extending nano4M with SAM segmentation tokens as a fifth modality. The project asks a simple question: can a compact multimodal model learn segmentation tokens jointly with RGB, depth, normals, and text in a unified masked modeling framework?

title: Overview
text:
Key takeaways

SAM tokens can be integrated into nano4M without changing the core model architecture.
The model is trained from scratch, not warm-started from a previous checkpoint.
The “offset” in this project refers to the token index range in the data representation, not offset training.
SAM reaches competitive validation loss relative to the other visual modalities on synthetic CLEVR scenes, although this number is partly helped by the simplicity of the masks and the large number of padding tokens.

title: Overview
text:
What was added
The extension adds a SAM-based segmentation modality to the existing multiCLEVR pipeline. RGB scenes are pseudo-labeled with SAM 2.1, converted into instance masks, normalized into fixed-length token sequences, and loaded alongside the original modalities.

title: Overview
text:
What this website shows
This site complements the report with a shorter narrative, result snapshots, and a clearer explanation of what the extension adds to nano4M. It is designed to be self-contained, so a reader can understand the high-level idea without opening the PDF first. It also clarifies the main limitation of the result: stable multimodal integration was successful, but absolute generation quality remains mixed.

title: Report
text:
This website presents the final COM-304 project SAM x Nano4M, developed by Laïne Satou Diongue, Nicola Rauseo, and Shabnam Tajik.
The project extends nano4M with a fifth modality: SAM segmentation tokens. The goal is not to build a specialized segmentation model, but to test whether segmentation tokens can be integrated into a compact multimodal masked modeling framework and co-trained jointly with RGB, depth, normals, and scene descriptions.
Across the site, we summarize the method, key quantitative results, and qualitative examples. The full written report is available here: Download the report
The central conclusion is that SAM integrates cleanly into nano4M and trains stably with the other modalities, but absolute generation quality remains limited by tokenization bottlenecks, model scale, and training budget.
